title: Do not use color as the only differentiator for badges and indicators
summary: Models use color exclusively — red for error, green for success, yellow for warning — in badges, status indicators, and notification dots without adding text, icon, or pattern labels, making the signal inaccessible to color-blind users and ambiguous when colors are similar.
instruction: Every status indicator, badge, or notification signal must use at least one non-color differentiator in addition to color: a text label, a distinct icon, a pattern fill, or a text abbreviation. Color alone is never sufficient. For badges with text, the text must describe the state (e.g., 'Active', 'Pending', 'Failed'), not just the category.
rationale: Approximately 8% of men and 0.5% of women have some form of color vision deficiency. Red-green differentiation is the most common form. A green 'Active' badge and a red 'Error' badge are indistinguishable to a deuteranope. Adding a checkmark icon for success and an X icon for error makes the state universally readable.
violations: A status badge using only green fill for 'Active' and red fill for 'Inactive' — no text, no icon, the badge is just a colored circle.
A table with a 'Status' column using red/yellow/green filled circles only — the user must remember which color maps to which state.
A notification dot (red circle on an icon) with no count text, no aria-label, and no tooltip — the user sees 'something is new' but not what or where.
controls: A status badge with green background + white checkmark icon + 'Active' text — three redundant cues (color, icon, text).
A table status column with colored indicator dots plus text labels: green + 'Active', yellow + 'Pending', red + 'Failed' — color and text together.
A notification badge with the count in white text on a red circle plus aria-label '3 unread notifications' — the number provides the 'how many', the aria-label provides the 'what'.
borderlineCases: A traffic-light-style status indicator used in a control room where operators are trained on the color mapping and the system includes a legend — acceptable in specialized contexts.
contextualExceptions: Ambient status indicators on hardware dashboards (factory floor, network operations center) where operators are trained on color codes and alternative signals are provided via alarms.
Accessibility settings mode where the user has enabled 'color-only mode' as a personal preference override.
Minimal data visualization sparklines where the color IS the data (e.g., red-to-green heatmap) with a legend provided.
detectorSignal: Colored element with a status/indicator role that contains no text node, no img/svg icon, and no aria-label describing the state
matcherProposal: Identify badge and indicator elements: [class*=badge], [class*=status], [class*=indicator], [class*=tag], small elements with colored backgrounds, circular elements < 32px with non-white background. For each, check: (a) contains text content, (b) contains element, (c) has aria-label or aria-labelledby. Flag if none of (a), (b), or (c) are true.
detectorLimitations: Cant distinguish between a decorative colored dot and a semantic status indicator without contextual role analysis.
Some status indicators use CSS pseudo-elements (::before, ::after) for the icon — these are not captured by child-element checks.
Status indicators that inherit their color from a parent (e.g., a row-level color) require parent-child color attribution.
falsePositiveScenarios: A purely decorative colored dot in a card header that has no semantic meaning — the detector may flag it as a status indicator.
A color swatch in a UI color picker where the color itself IS the content — the swatch is not a status indicator.
mitigationStrategy: Only flag elements that have semantic indicators in their class list or are inside status/table/data-display contexts. Exclude decorative elements (aria-hidden=true, role=presentation, or inside color-picker contexts).

fixturePlans.violation: A user table with a 'Status' column that shows solid green circles for active and solid red circles for inactive — no text, no icons, no aria-labels.
fixturePlans.control: A user table with a 'Status' column showing green circle + white checkmark + 'Active' text (red circle + X + 'Inactive') — color, icon, and text combined.
fixturePlans.borderline: A server-room monitoring panel using red/yellow/green status lights as ambient indicators, with a printed legend at the bottom of the panel and operator training on color codes.

cleanRoomAttestations: Developed without access to proprietary prompt collections.
Color vision deficiency prevalence statistics based on publicly available medical data.
overlapNotes: First badge-indicator proposal. No overlap with existing rules.
